points.title: Lack of methodological analysis and theoretical grounding
points.detail: The paper is presented as a literature review but contains no critical methodological analysis. It merely summarizes architectural changes (e.g., 'added Batch Normalization', 'introduced FPN') without evaluating the theoretical soundness of these choices, such as why anchor clustering improves localization or how multi-scale detection resolves the scale variance problem from a statistical learning perspective. There is no discussion of loss function design, convergence guarantees, or bias-variance tradeoffs. This fails the rigor expected of a methodological review.

points.title: No statistical validity or experimental reproducibility assessment
points.detail: The review reports performance numbers (e.g., '63.4% mAP', '57.9% AP50') without any discussion of statistical significance, variance across runs, dataset biases, or evaluation protocols. There is no analysis of whether improvements are statistically significant or merely due to hyperparameter tuning. The paper does not provide any reproducibility details such as training configurations, data splits, or hardware used, which is essential for a methodological review.

points.title: No original contribution or critical synthesis
points.detail: The paper is a purely descriptive chronological summary of YOLO versions, lacking any original analysis, comparative theoretical framework, or novel insight. It does not identify contradictions, unresolved issues, or propose a unified theoretical perspective. For a rigorous academic venue, a review must offer more than a list of incremental changes; it should critically evaluate the evolution of ideas and their theoretical underpinnings.

points.title: Incomplete and outdated coverage
points.detail: The review claims to cover up to YOLOv13 but only discusses YOLOv1 through YOLOv4, with the YOLOv4 section cut off mid-sentence. This is a fatal flaw for a literature review claiming comprehensive coverage. The paper also omits critical later developments such as YOLOv5, YOLOX, YOLOv7, YOLOv8, and their methodological innovations (e.g., decoupled heads, anchor-free detection, dynamic label assignment).

points.title: Internal inconsistency in describing YOLOv1 limitations
points.detail: The review states YOLOv1 'can only recognize one category' per grid cell, which is correct, but then later praises YOLOv3 for using binary cross-entropy for multi-label classification without explaining how this resolves the limitation. The logical connection between the problem and the proposed solution is missing, weakening the internal consistency of the argument.

points.title: No experimental results or evaluation section
points.detail: The paper is a literature review and contains no original experiments, no quantitative results, no datasets, no implementation details, and no evaluation metrics. There is no experimental setup to assess for reproducibility or statistical validity.

points.title: No baseline comparisons
points.detail: As a literature review, the paper does not conduct any comparative experiments. It merely cites reported performance numbers from original papers (e.g., YOLOv1 at 63.4% mAP on VOC2007) without any controlled re-implementation or fair comparison under a unified experimental setup.

points.title: No ablation studies
points.detail: The paper does not perform any ablation studies to validate the contribution of individual components. It only describes innovations (e.g., Batch Normalization, anchor boxes, FPN) as reported in original works, without any controlled experiments to isolate their effects.

points.title: No implementation details for reproducibility
points.detail: The paper lacks any information about hardware, software frameworks, hyperparameters, training configurations, or data preprocessing that would allow reproduction of any claimed results. It is purely a historical narrative.

points.title: No statistical significance or error analysis
points.detail: The paper reports single-point performance numbers (e.g., mAP, FPS) without any confidence intervals, standard deviations, or error bars. There is no discussion of variance across runs, dataset splits, or statistical significance of improvements between versions.

points.title: Inconsistent citation style
points.detail: The paper uses a mix of parenthetical and narrative citations inconsistently (e.g., '（Redmon et al., 2016）' vs. 'Redmon & Farhadi, 2017'). For a rigorous venue, a single, consistent citation format (e.g., APA or IEEE) should be used throughout.

points.title: Missing figure/table captions and labels
points.detail: The paper contains no figures, tables, or their corresponding captions and labels. For a survey paper, architectural diagrams, performance comparison tables, and timeline figures are essential for clarity. Their absence significantly reduces readability and reproducibility of the narrative.

points.title: Insufficient positioning against prior art
points.detail: The paper briefly mentions two-stage detectors (R-CNN, Fast/Faster R-CNN) in Section 2.1 but fails to systematically compare YOLO's contributions against other contemporary single-stage detectors (e.g., SSD, RetinaNet) or later competing frameworks (e.g., DETR, ViT-based detectors). This omission weakens the survey's scholarly value, as it does not establish why YOLO's specific innovations were significant relative to the broader field.

points.title: Lack of discussion on YOLO variants and forks
points.detail: The paper stops at YOLOv4 and does not cover the numerous influential YOLO variants and forks (e.g., YOLOv5, YOLOX, YOLOv6, YOLOv7, YOLOv8, PP-YOLO, etc.) that have been published in the last 3-4 years. Given the title claims coverage up to 'YOLOv13', this omission is a fatal gap. The paper also fails to discuss the broader 'related work' of object detection beyond YOLO, such as transformer-based detectors or anchor-free methods.

points.title: Abrupt and incomplete ending
points.detail: The paper ends mid-sentence after introducing YOLOv4, with no conclusion, future outlook, or references section. This makes the manuscript appear as an unfinished draft, severely impacting its presentation clarity and scholarly completeness.
